The ANSI UASSC’s mission is to coordinate and accelerate the development of the standards and conformity assessment programs needed to facilitate the safe integration of unmanned aircraft systems (UAS) – commonly known as drones – into the national airspace system (NAS) of the United States.

# CODE: Collaborative Operations in Denied Environment

#### Summary

![](/sites/default/files/styles/wide_816/public/Code-619-316.jpg?itok=PyuOv5TF)






The U.S. militaryâs investments in unmanned aircraft systems (UAS) have proven invaluable for missions ranging from intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance (ISR) to tactical strike, but most current systems demand continuous control by a dedicated pilot and sensor operator supported by numerous telemetry-linked analysts.

This requirement severely limits the scalability and cost-effectiveness of UAS operations and compounds the operational challenges posed by dynamic, remote engagements against highly mobile targets in contested electromagnetic environments.

DARPAâs Collaborative Operations in Denied Environment (CODE) program aims to overcome these limitations with new algorithms and software for existing unmanned aircraft that would extend mission capabilities and improve U.S. forcesâ ability to conduct operations in denied or contested airspace. CODE researchers seek to create a modular software architecture beyond the current state of the art that is resilient to bandwidth limitations and communications disruptions yet compatible with existing standards and amenable to affordable retrofit into existing platforms.

CODE intends to focus in particular on developing and demonstrating improvements in collaborative autonomyâthe capability of groups of UAS to work together under a single personâs supervisory control. The unmanned vehicles would continuously evaluate their own states and environments and present recommendations for coordinated UAS actions to a mission supervisor, who would approve or disapprove such team actions and direct any mission changes. Using collaborative autonomy, CODE-enabled unmanned aircraft would find targets and engage them as appropriate under established rules of engagement, leverage nearby CODE-equipped systems with minimal supervision, and adapt to dynamic situations such as attrition of friendly forces or the emergence of unanticipated threats.

CODEâs envisioned improvements to collaborative autonomy would help transform UAS operations from requiring multiple operators for each UAS to having one mission commander simultaneously directing all of the unmanned vehicles required for the mission. Commanders could mix and match different systems with specific capabilities to suit individual missions instead of depending on a single UAS with integrated capabilities, the loss of which would be potentially catastrophic. This flexibility could significantly increase the mission- and cost-effectiveness of legacy assets, reduce development times and costs for future systems, and enable new deployment concepts.

The program plans to develop the operational concepts for CODE-enabled strike missions and validate their effectiveness through detailed modeling and simulation. It also intends to develop the most promising capabilities and demonstrate them in flight using multiple surrogate UAS equipped with mesh network radios and a variety of payloads, augmented by a number of virtual UAS.

Regulatory, technical, and safety considerations for UAV-based inspection in chemical process plants: A systematic review of current practice and future directions

Highlights
----------

*   •Investigate regulatory frameworks for drone inspections in chemical process plants. 
*   •Compare cross-jurisdictional regulatory variations and safety/compliance implications. 
*   •Assess safety challenges of drone operations in hazardous chemical environments. 
*   •Propose future research directions and enhancements in regulations, technology, and safety protocols. 

Abstract
--------

Chemical process plants require frequent inspection to ensure operational safety and prevent equipment failure. In recent years, the use of UAVs for inspecting chemical process plants has gained significant attention due to their ability to provide more efficient, cost-effective, and safer inspection services. This review article provides a comprehensive analysis of the current practice and future directions of UAV-based inspection in chemical process plants, with a focus on the regulatory, technical, and safety considerations. The review searched for studies on UAV standards in chemical process plants using multiple databases and specific keywords. Two stages of search were conducted, including a full-text review, and inclusion and exclusion criteria were applied to select relevant studies published in English between 2013 and 2023. Data on UAV standards were extracted, including benefits, limitations, national as well as international standards, challenges, and opportunities. A narrative synthesis approach was used to summarize the findings of the studies in a descriptive manner. The implications of this review are significant, as they provide a comprehensive understanding of the benefits and limitations of using UAVs for inspection in chemical process plants. The review highlights the need for continued research and development in this area to address technical and safety challenges and to facilitate wider adoption of UAV-based inspection technologies in chemical process plants.

1. Introduction
---------------

In the chemical process plants industry, Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) are increasingly employed for hazard inspection, offering advantages such as cost savings, increased efficiency, and improved safety ([Kas and Johnson, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#b0005)). As defined by the International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO), an Unmanned Aircraft System (UAS) involves the operation of a remotely-piloted aircraft without pilot intervention in flight management. This operational definition highlights the capability of UAVs to conduct inspections without jeopardizing the safety and health of employees, a significant improvement over traditional methods ([Wankmüller et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#b0010)). UAV-based inspections not only enhance safety but also contribute to faster, more efficient, and precise assessments, reducing the likelihood of accidents. Equipped with advanced cameras and sensors, UAVs can access hazardous and challenging areas, including storage tanks and flare stacks, to detect early signs of dangerous conditions such as spills or leaks. This early detection minimizes the severity of incidents and mitigates their adverse impact on the environment ([Godfrey and Sibaja Brenes, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#b0015)).

Despite their benefits, the use of UAVs in chemical process plants poses some risks to operators and facilities. UAVs can lose control and communication due to electromagnetic signal disruption, leading to collisions with plant equipment that can damage facilities and threaten personnel safety ([Solodov et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#b0020)). Additionally, the use of UAVs may raise privacy and security concerns, as they have the potential to collect private information or interfere with plant operations if not appropriately secured ([Finn and Wright, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#b0025)). Moreover, there is a risk of cyberattacks, as UAVs are vulnerable to hacking that could compromise the security and confidentiality of plant data ([Hadi et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#b0030)).

While it is true that the use of UAVs in chemical plants offers numerous benefits, such as improved safety, increased efficiency, and cost savings, these advantages can be overshadowed by the risks associated with their use if proper safety measures and standardization are not in place. The unique hazards and requirements of chemical process plants necessitate the development and implementation of UAV standards to ensure responsible UAV operations. Standardized safety procedures, operator training and certification, and secure communication and cybersecurity measures are crucial to mitigate the risks and ensure effective UAV operations. Given the limited research on UAV standards in chemical process plants, it is essential to prioritize and invest in this area to promote safer and more efficient industrial operations.

Although there are many UAV standards and regulations available that can be adapted and applied to the chemical process plant industry, the lack of specific standards or regulations for UAV use in chemical processing plants remains a concern. While documents such as the Manual on Remotely Piloted Aircraft Systems (RPAS) established by the International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) in 2015 ([I. c. a., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#b0035)) and the guidelines for UAV operation developed by the American Petroleum Institute (API) in 2019 ([Institute, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#b0040)) cover important aspects for operators, debates and controversies persist regarding UAV standards in chemical plants, particularly related to safety. Therefore, safety regulations and adequate training for UAV operators are necessary to prevent threats to the lives of individuals and assets in chemical plants.

The objective of this review paper is to assess the status of UAV standards and regulations, both generally and for chemical plants, to identify opportunities for enhancement. The evaluation will entail a methodical examination of published literature and official regulations from diverse jurisdictions, emphasizing regulatory and safety frameworks, as well as technical considerations in utilizing UAV-based inspections for chemical process plants. The conclusions of this review will furnish policy makers and industry practitioners with valuable insights, acting as a guide for the creation of UAV inspection systems that can enhance the safety and efficiency of inspections of chemical process plants.

The structure of this paper is as follows. In [Section 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#s0010), the methodology used to conduct the literature study is described. [Section 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#s0015) presents a comprehensive review of existing UAV standards and regulations at national and international levels from various jurisdictions. [Section 4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#s0055) evaluates and analyses the strengths and weaknesses of the existing standards while identifying gaps and inconsistencies. In [Section 5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#s0080), the paper addresses the key challenges and barriers to developing and implementing effective UAV standards and explores opportunities for enhancing UAV standards for chemical process plants. [Section 6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#s0150) outlines future research and practice directions related to UAV standards in chemical process plants, including recommendations for improving existing standards and developing new standards and regulations. Finally, [Section 7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#s0195) concludes the paper.

2. Methodology
--------------

This systematic review employed a comprehensive search strategy to identify relevant literature on UAV standards in chemical process plants. We aimed to capture a broad range of perspectives and insights using five academic databases: IEEE Xplore, ScienceDirect, Google Scholar, Research Gate, and Springer. Our initial search query combined core keywords with logical operators to balance comprehensiveness and specificity. Specifically, we used “UAV OR UAV” to encompass both terms, AND them with “chemical” to focus on the relevant context, and then further refined the search with additional relevant terms like “standardization,” “regulations,” and “safety.” This initial search yielded 31,459 papers.

To ensure the selected studies directly addressed our research question, we applied inclusion and exclusion criteria at two stages. In the first stage, titles and abstracts were screened based on the following criteria:
*   •Focus on UAV Standards: Studies had to primarily discuss standards or regulations related to UAV use in chemical process plants. 
*   •English Language: We limited the review to English-language publications for consistency and accessibility. 
*   •Publication Date: Studies published between 2013 and 2023 were considered to capture the latest developments in UAV technology and standards. 

This preliminary screening excluded 24,932 papers, with keywords like “agricultural” or “construction” indicating non-alignment with our research focus.

In the second stage, we conducted a full-text review of the remaining studies to definitively assess their eligibility. Studies were excluded if they:
*   •Did not meet the previously mentioned inclusion criteria. 
*   •Duplicated content or findings from already included studies. 
*   •Primarily focused on theoretical discussions or conceptual frameworks without empirical data or case studies. 
*   •Descriptive UAV control algorithm or predictive technique 

This final stage excluded 4,937 papers due to reasons such as being opinion pieces or discussing UAV applications outside the chemical industry. From the final 85 studies included in the review, we extracted information related to UAV standards in chemical process plants. This included:
*   •The perceived importance and benefits of UAVs in this context. 
*   •Identified gaps and limitations in existing standards and regulations. 
*   •Specific national and international UAV standards from major players like the European Union, United States, Germany, Japan, and Malaysia. 
*   •Challenges and opportunities associated with implementing UAV standards in the chemical industry. 

We adopted a narrative synthesis approach to analyze the extracted data. This entailed systematically reviewing and summarizing the findings of each study to identify key themes, patterns, and potential discrepancies. The synthesized findings are presented and discussed in detail in the following sections of this paper.

3. Current state of uav standards
---------------------------------

This section provides a comprehensive review of existing UAV standards and regulations at national and international levels from various jurisdictions. The current state of UAV standards differs significantly among different countries and regions. While some countries such as the United States, European Union, Germany, and Japan have established standards and regulations pertaining to the use of UAVs, others still lack detailed and extensive frameworks. International organizations such as the International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) and the European Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) have established proper regulations and policies for general UAV operations.

Apart from that, on the international level, the US Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) has created general UAV standards that mandate remote pilot certification for commercial UAV operators as well as adherence to operational restrictions including flying within visible line of vision at all times as well as refraining from flying over civilians. Besides, American Petroleum Institute (API) has published guidelines for operating unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) in 2019, particularly for the oil and gas industry where it can be adapted for chemical process plants. It covers topics including risk management, developing standard operating procedures, data security, and emergency preparation, outlining crucial elements for the operators ([Finn and Wright, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#b0025)). Considering from the national level perspectives, in Malaysia, the country’s civil aviation technical sector regulatory body, Civil Aviation Authority of Malaysia (CAAM) has established general laws and regulations related to UAV operations in 2016 which is bound to Civil Aviation Regulation 2016 (MCAR) Regulation 140–144. The regulations incorporate UAV registration, pilot training, restricted areas, flight restrictions, UAV insurance, and payload restrictions.

In this section, we will examine six key UAV standards, including the International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) UAV Regulation, European Union (EU) UAV Standards/European Aviation Safety Agency (EASA), United States (US) UAV Standards/US Federal Aviation Administration (FAA), Japan UAV Standards, and Civil Aviation Authority of Malaysia (CAAM) UAV Regulation. These standards and regulations have been selected based on their relevance to the use of UAVs in chemical process plants, and their ability to provide guidance for regulatory, safety, and technical considerations in implementing UAV-based inspections.

### 3.1. International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) UAV Regulation

The International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) is a specialized organization within the United Nations (UN) that is responsible for promoting safe and organized development of international civil aviation on a global scale. It was established in December 1944 by the Convention on International Civil Aviation, commonly known as the Chicago Convention ([Abeyratne, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#b0045)).

With 193 member nations, the ICAO has developed a range of international regulations, recommended practices, and legislation to address aviation safety, operational hazards, and international priorities ([Shin, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#b0050)).

The ICAO has developed three important documents related to UAVs as shown in [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#t0005). As described in [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#t0005), one of the key documents produced by the ICAO in this regard is the ICAO Model Unmanned Aircraft System (UAS) Regulations. These regulations were developed with the aim of providing a template for member states to implement or supplement their existing UAS regulations, with a focus on ensuring the safe, secure, and efficient integration of unmanned aircraft into the global aviation system. The regulations are divided into three parts: Part 101 covers general provisions for UAS operations, including definitions and operational limitations; Part 102 covers UAS operator certification and training requirements; and Part 149 covers UAS manufacturing and design standards ([Henderson, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#b0055)).

Table 1. Summary the three documents related to UAVs developed by the ICAO.

| Document | Description | Coverage |
| --- | --- | --- |
| ICAO Model Unmanned Aircraft System (UAS) Regulations | Template for member states to implement or supplement existing UAS regulations, with a focus on ensuring safe, secure, and efficient integration of unmanned aircraft into the global aviation system. | Part 101 covers general provisions for UAS operations, including definitions and operational limitations; Part 102 covers UAS operator certification and training requirements; and Part 149 covers UAS manufacturing and design standards |
| ICAO Circular 328 | Provides guidance on the integration of unmanned aircraft systems into non-segregated airspace and at aerodromes | Covers fundamental differences between UAS and manned aviation and provides guidance on how to address these differences to ensure safe and efficient UAS operations. |
| ICAO Manual on Remotely Piloted Aircraft System (RPAS) | Provides guidance on the integration of remotely piloted aircraft systems (RPAS) into non-segregated airspace and at aerodromes; intended to be used as a reference by states in developing their own national regulations and procedures | Covers a wider range of topics than Cir-328, including RPA operations, aircraft and systems, personnel obligations, and recommendations for the development of national RPAS regulations and procedures |

In addition to the ICAO Model UAS Regulations, the ICAO has also produced the ICAO Circular 328, which provides guidance on the integration of unmanned aircraft systems into non-segregated airspace (airspace where both manned and unmanned aircraft operate without any physical separation) and at aerodromes (area on land or water that is intended to be used either wholly or in part for the arrival, departure, and surface movement of aircraft) ([Raduntsev et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#b0060)).

This document considers the fundamental differences between UAS and manned aviation and provides guidance on how these differences can be addressed in order to ensure safe and efficient UAS operations. Finally, the ICAO Manual on Remotely Piloted Aircraft System (RPAS) provides guidance on the integration of remotely piloted aircraft systems (RPAS) into non-segregated airspace and at aerodromes ([I. c. a., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#b0035)). The ICAO Manual on RPAS is intended to be used as a reference by States in developing their own national regulations and procedures, while Cir-328 provides high-level guidance and recommended practices ([Abeyratne, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#b0065)).

### 3.2. European Union (EU) UAV standards/European Aviation safety Agency (EASA)

The European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) has established regulations and standards for the safe operation of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) in European airspace. These standards are designed to mitigate risks associated with the use of UAVs and ensure their safe integration into the European aviation system. EU Regulations 2019/947 and 2019/945, which provide the regulatory framework for the legal use of civilian unmanned aircraft in European airspace, are subject to different adoption processes ([Pūraitė and Šilinskė, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#b0070)). Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2019/947 outlines rules and procedures for the operation of unmanned aircraft, including technical requirements, operational limitations, and responsibilities of operators and remote pilots. Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2019/945, on the other hand, covers unmanned aircraft systems (UAS) and third-country operators of UAS, setting out requirements for UAS operations and operator responsibilities ([Bassi, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#b0075)).

EASA's policymaking process for UAVs is based on a risk-based approach that does not differentiate between leisure and business-related operations ([Konert and Dunin, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#b0080)). Instead, weight, specifications, and intended activity are considered in assessing risk. Most civil UAV activities and risks are covered under Regulation (EU) 2019/947, which has been effective in 27 European Union countries since December 31, 2020, including Switzerland, Iceland, Liechtenstein, and Norway ([Rakotonarivo et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#b0085)). The regulation classifies civil UAVs into three categories: open, specific, and certified. The open category covers low-risk civil UAV activities, requiring no authorization if the pilot complies with necessary regulations. In contrast, the specific category is for riskier activities and requires national authority approval after a risk assessment is carried out to ensure safety.

The certified category carries the highest risk and requires certification of both pilot and UAV to ensure safety. [Table 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590198225000223#t0010) presents a summary of the key criteria related to the regulations. Under Regulation (EU) 2019/947, autonomous UAVs are permitted to navigate safely without a pilot, as they utilize artificial intelligence to manage unforeseen crises.

Table 2. Summary of EASA Regulations.

| Empty Cell | Open Category | Specific Category | Certified Category |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Requirements for Respective Category** |
| Maximum Take-Off Mass (MTOM) | < 25 kg | N/A | N/A |
| Within Visual Line of Sight (VLOS) / Beyond Visual Line of Sight (BVLOS) | VLOS | VLOS or BVLOS (depending on operation) | VLOS or BVLOS |
| Carriage of Dangerous Goods | Prohibited | Prohibited | Allowed |
| Carriage of People | Not Allowed | Not Allowed | Allowed |
| Operation Over People Crowd | Not Allowed | Depending on Operation | Allowed |
| Maximum Flight Altitude | < 120 m | N/A | N/A |
| **Requirements for Operation** |
| Requirement of Prior Authorization or Declaration | No | Depending on Operation | Authorization |
| Pilot Certification | Theoretical Knowledge Examination (Online)+Potentially Self-Practical Training | The operational authorization or the appropriate standard scenario shall determine the training. | High requirements – comparable to pilot license in manned aviation |

In contrast, automatic UAVs require pre-determined flight paths, and pilots must respond to unexpected situations. While automatic UAVs are permitted in all categories, autonomous UAVs are not allowed in the open category. UAV owners and operators are required to register with the National Aviation Authority of the EU country in which they reside, with registration required only once and needing renewal after a certain period. However, registration is not required for small UAVs weighing less than 250 g or toys that conform to specific regulations. Certification is not mandatory for UAVs in the open category and is only necessary when the operation is deemed dangerous or risky. The registration number must be displayed on the UAV, and a green flashing light is required for night flights. Restrictions on geographical zones where UAVs are prohibited will be declared by each member state. In the open category, UAVs weighing more than 250 g are not allowed to fly above citizens and flying within 30 m horizontally or flying over uninvolved individuals is prohibited, except when low-speed mode is enabled.

Drones
======

The future of aviation is here. The FAA collaborates with industry and communities to advance drone operations and integrate them into the national airspace. Whether you are flying for fun or work, get the rules, resources, and tools, to help you fly safely.

[We Can Help You!](https://www.faa.gov/uas/getting_started)

All drone pilots, new or experienced, benefit from rules and safety tips to help you fly safe. More importantly, drone safety is the law. Find relevant resources below according to how you use your drone. Whether you’re a [public safety drone operator](https://www.faa.gov/uas/public_safety_gov), [certificated remote pilot](https://www.faa.gov/uas/commercial_operators/become_a_drone_pilot/), [model aircraft flyer](https://www.faa.gov/uas/recreational_flyers), or a[drone racer](https://www.faa.gov/uas/recreational_flyers), you’re in the right place to learn about integrating these new entrants into our national airspace system.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Regulatory bodies like the ANSI UASSC work together with industry partners to establish comprehensive UAV standards, ensuring safe integration into national airspace.
Ongoing R&D initiatives, such as DARPA's CODE program, focus on enhancing UAV capabilities and addressing operational challenges through innovative algorithms and autonomous systems.
Regulatory frameworks are regularly updated to incorporate emerging technologies and insights, ensuring that UAV standards remain relevant and effective for evolving airspace dynamics.
Adaptive regulatory strategies are necessary to respond to the rapid advancements in UAV technology, ensuring safety while fostering innovation and operational flexibility.
Engaging diverse stakeholders, including government agencies, industry experts, and community members, is crucial for developing balanced UAV regulations that address safety and operational needs.
